annotations.label: Solvable by radicals

anchor.snippet: an equation is by definition solvable by radicals if its roots may be expressed by a formula involving only integers

annotations.note: `solvableByRad F E` is the intermediate field of elements obtained from `F` by field operations and `n`-th roots, formalizing solvability by radicals.

annotations.label: Abel–Ruffini theorem (lead statement)

anchor.snippet: which asserts that a general polynomial of degree at least five cannot be solved by radicals

annotations.note: Only the easy direction `solvableByRad ⇒ IsSolvable p.Gal` is in Mathlib; the explicit statement that the general degree-≥5 polynomial is not solvable by radicals is not present.

annotations.label: Discriminant detects multiple/real roots

anchor.snippet: the discriminant is a symmetric function in the roots that reflects properties of the roots

annotations.note: Cubic discriminant detects distinct roots; general discriminants (`Algebra.discr`, `Polynomial.discriminant`) exist but the broad symmetric-function/root-detection statement is only formalized in special cases.

annotations.label: Abel–Ruffini theorem (historical)

anchor.snippet: The quintic was almost proven to have no general solutions by radicals by Paolo Ruffini in 1799

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes one direction of Abel–Ruffini (root solvable by radicals ⇒ Galois group solvable); a concrete unsolvable quintic witnessing impossibility is not formalized.

annotations.label: Galois group of a polynomial (permutation group)

anchor.snippet: These permutations together form a permutation group , also called the Galois group of the polynomial

annotations.note: `Polynomial.Gal p` is the Galois group of `p`, and `Polynomial.Gal.galActionHom` embeds it into permutations of the roots.

annotations.label: Galois group of x^2 − 4x + 1

anchor.snippet: We conclude that the Galois group of the polynomial x 2 − 4 x + 1 consists of two permutations

annotations.note: No worked example computing `(X^2 - 4*X + 1).Gal` appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quartic with Galois group the Klein four-group

anchor.snippet: Galois theory implies that, since the polynomial is irreducible, the Galois group has at least four elements

annotations.note: Mathlib has `KleinFour` and `Polynomial.Gal`, but no worked quartic example identifying its Galois group with the Klein four-group.

annotations.label: Modern Galois group of a field extension

anchor.snippet: In the modern approach, one starts with a field extension L / K

annotations.note: `IsGalois F E` (separable + normal) together with the `Gal(E/F) := E ≃ₐ[F] E` notation captures the modern definition.

annotations.label: Absolute Galois group of Q

anchor.snippet: the absolute Galois group of Q , defined to be the Galois group of K / Q where K is an algebraic closure of Q

annotations.note: `Field.absoluteGaloisGroup K := AlgebraicClosure K ≃ₐ[K] AlgebraicClosure K` defines the absolute Galois group of any field; specializing to ℚ gives the article's notion.

annotations.label: Radical extension via cyclic factor groups

anchor.section: Solvable groups and solution by radicals
anchor.snippet: then it is a radical extension and the elements of L can then be expressed using the n th root of some element of K

annotations.note: Mathlib captures the radical closure via `solvableByRad`, but does not provide a standalone `IsRadicalExtension` predicate built from cyclic factor groups.

annotations.label: Solvable Galois group

anchor.section: Solvable groups and solution by radicals
anchor.snippet: If all the factor groups in its composition series are cyclic, the Galois group is called solvable

annotations.note: `IsSolvable G` (via the derived series) applied to `p.Gal` or `Gal(E/F)` formalizes the notion of a solvable Galois group.

annotations.label: Existence of non-solvable polynomials for n > 4

anchor.section: Solvable groups and solution by radicals
anchor.snippet: for every n > 4 , there exist polynomials of degree n which are not solvable by radicals

annotations.note: No theorem in Mathlib asserts existence of degree-`n` polynomials (for each `n > 4`) that are not solvable by radicals.

annotations.label: Symmetric group S_n is not solvable for n ≥ 5

anchor.section: Solvable groups and solution by radicals
anchor.snippet: the symmetric group S n contains a simple , noncyclic, normal subgroup , namely the alternating group A n

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `Equiv.Perm.not_solvable` for cardinality ≥ 5, via simplicity of the alternating group `alternatingGroup.isSimple` for n ≥ 5.

annotations.label: Non-solvable quintic x^5 − x − 1

anchor.snippet: Van der Waerden [ 11 ] cites the polynomial f ( x ) = x 5 − x − 1

annotations.note: Mathlib has no proof that `X^5 - X - 1` (or any specific quintic) has non-solvable Galois group.

annotations.label: Inverse Galois problem

anchor.snippet: The inverse Galois problem is to find a field extension with a given Galois group

annotations.note: No formal statement or namespace for the inverse Galois problem appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Every finite group occurs as a Galois group (over some base)

anchor.snippet: all finite groups do occur as Galois groups

annotations.note: Mathlib does not state that every finite group is realized as a Galois group of some field extension.

annotations.label: Cayley's theorem

anchor.snippet: Cayley's theorem says that G is (up to isomorphism) a subgroup of the symmetric group S on the elements of G

annotations.note: Cayley's theorem is realized via the left-regular action embedding `G ↪ Equiv.Perm G`; see also `Subgroup.equivMapOfInjective` and the standard embedding into `Equiv.Perm`.

annotations.label: Shafarevich: solvable finite groups over Q

anchor.snippet: Igor Shafarevich proved that every solvable finite group is the Galois group of some extension of Q

annotations.note: Shafarevich's theorem on realizing solvable finite groups over ℚ is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jacobson correspondence for purely inseparable extensions

anchor.snippet: Jacobson (1944) showed that this establishes a one-to-one correspondence

annotations.note: Mathlib has substantial `PurelyInseparable` API but no formalization of Jacobson's bijection between intermediate fields and restricted Lie algebras of derivations.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of Galois theory

anchor.snippet: this connection, the fundamental theorem of Galois theory , allows reducing certain problems in field theory to group theory

annotations.note: The Galois correspondence between intermediate fields of `E/F` and subgroups of `Gal(E/F)` is formalized as `IsGalois.intermediateFieldEquivSubgroup` (with companion lemmas about fixed fields and orders).

annotations.label: Purely inseparable extension

anchor.snippet: General field extensions can be split into a separable, followed by a purely inseparable field extension

annotations.note: `IsPurelyInseparable F E` formalizes purely inseparable extensions; the separable/purely-inseparable decomposition is available via the separable closure inside `E`.

annotations.label: Constructible regular polygons (Gauss–Wantzel)

anchor.snippet: characterizing the regular polygons that are constructible

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain the Gauss–Wantzel characterization of constructible regular n-gons (n a product of a power of 2 and distinct Fermat primes).

annotations.label: Impossibility of doubling the cube and trisecting the angle

anchor.snippet: doubling the cube and trisecting the angle

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks formalizations of the classical impossibility results for doubling the cube and trisecting an arbitrary angle by compass and straightedge.

annotations.label: Elementary symmetric polynomials

anchor.snippet: the coefficients of a monic polynomial are ( up to sign) the elementary symmetric polynomials in the roots

annotations.note: `MvPolynomial.esymm σ R n` formalizes the n-th elementary symmetric polynomial; Vieta-type identities relating these to coefficients of `∏ (X - root i)` are available as `Multiset.prod_X_sub_C_coeff` and friends.

annotations.label: Klein four-group

anchor.snippet: They form a group that is isomorphic to the Klein four-group

annotations.note: `IsKleinFour G` axiomatizes the Klein four-group; concrete instances `ZMod 2 × ZMod 2` are provided.

annotations.label: Composition series of a group

anchor.section: Solvable groups and solution by radicals
anchor.snippet: each field extension L / K corresponds to a factor group in a composition series of the Galois group

annotations.note: `CompositionSeries` formalizes composition series in a general lattice setting; specializing to the subgroup lattice gives composition series of groups, with Jordan–Hölder available.

annotations.label: Intermediate field of L/K

anchor.snippet: In the modern approach, one starts with a field extension L / K (read " L over K "), and examines the group of automorphisms of L that fix K

annotations.note: `IntermediateField K L` is the lattice of intermediate fields of `L/K`, used as the field-side object in the Galois correspondence.

annotations.label: Derivation satisfying the Leibniz rule

anchor.snippet: K - linear endomorphisms of F satisfying the Leibniz rule

annotations.note: `Derivation R A M` formalizes `R`-linear maps satisfying the Leibniz rule; specializing to `M := F` with `R := K` gives the space of `K`-linear derivations of `F` used in the Jacobson correspondence.

annotations.label: Alternating group A_n is simple for n ≥ 5

anchor.section: Solvable groups and solution by radicals
anchor.snippet: the symmetric group S n contains a simple , noncyclic, normal subgroup , namely the alternating group A n

annotations.note: Simplicity of `alternatingGroup (Fin n)` for `n ≥ 5` is formalized; this is the key group-theoretic input behind `Equiv.Perm.not_solvable` and Abel–Ruffini.
